design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore ancient Greece map, enter dungeons as sidescrolling platformer-combat sections, defeat mythological enemies, collect upgrades and divine gifts from Greek gods, progress toward Hades in Tartarus
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (health, upgrades) balanced against combat encounters and platforming obstacles; narrative urgency of rescuing Helene from Hades
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Orpheus in classical Greek mythology, gaining favor from Olympian gods, wielding mythological weapons and artifacts, descending into the underworld to save a loved one
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Strong thematic commitment to Greek mythology with named locations (Arcadia, Argolis, Attica), specific mythological creatures (Minotaur, Cerberus, Nemean Lion), and narrative framing around divine patronage; non-linear dungeon access with optional side quests that unlock secrets; hybrid structure combining top-down exploration map with sidescrolling dungeon gameplay

difficulty.lives_system: Health bar system with upgrades available; no traditional lives documented
difficulty.checkpointing: Dungeons are discrete sidescrolling sections within the exploration framework; checkpointing structure not explicitly documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Optional side quests can be completed to gain secret items and power-ups that make progression easier; non-linear dungeon access allows players to tackle challenges in flexible order

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon upgrades (wooden club → Staff of Fennel → Nymph's Sword → Divine Sword with ranged capabilities); shield upgrades; health maximization items; speed upgrades; special equipment including Harp of Apollo, Sandals of Hermes; divine gifts granted by gods (Zeus initiates patronage chain)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Divine favor system: Zeus encourages other Olympian gods to grant Orpheus powers and weapons as progression gate; certain items (like beams from Divine Sword) require discovery of prerequisite items to avoid health damage
replayability_hooks: Optional side quests with secret items and power-ups
Multiple mythological creatures and boss encounters
Non-linear dungeon access allowing different play orders
Collection of three fragments of love as narrative collectibles

world_building_techniques: Named ancient Greek city-states as explorable locations on world map
Mythological creature encounters that reinforce setting authenticity
Direct use of Greek mythological artifacts (Harp of Apollo, Sword of Hephaestus, Staff of Prometheus, Sandals of Hermes)
Narrative framing through kidnapping rescue plot with emotional stakes (Helene taken by Hades)
Divine patronage hierarchy centered on Zeus and Olympian gods
Tartarus as underworld final destination reinforces mythological geography

boss_design.philosophy: Mythologically grounded encounter design featuring iconic creatures from Greek mythology; final confrontation with Hades structured as two-stage event combining shadow battle (supernatural trial) with direct confrontation (ultimate threat)
notable_bosses: Hades (shadow reflection phase, then true form)

faction_indicators: Olympian gods as patronage faction (Zeus, others unnamed)
Hades and underworld minions as opposing faction
Orpheus as protagonist isolated against mythological opposition
danger_signals: Kidnapping by underworld ruler establishes external threat
Progression into Tartarus requires confronting progressively dangerous mythological creatures
Health management against enemy encounters signals escalating danger
environmental_storytelling: Ancient Greek city-states on world map establish cultural context
Dungeon architecture within each location reflects location identity
Tartarus as final area represents hostile underworld environment
Mythological creature placement in specific dungeons ties enemies to locations
